commentCollected: Excessive Data Points: When a chart contains too many data points, it can overwhelm the viewer and make it difficult to discern meaningful patterns or trends. Cluttered Labels and Annotations: If labels, titles, and annotations are excessively crowded or overlapping, it can make it challenging to identify specific elements within the chart. Poor Color Choices: Using too many colors or poorly contrasting colors can make it difficult to differentiate between different data series or categories. Data Overload: Including too much data in a single chart or diagram can make it difficult to focus on the most important information and can obscure key insights.

The more complex picture uses differnet fonts in one "slide". This is confusing. The left picture instead applies one font only. 4. Magnification effects. Applying magnification effects to a slide or picture requires the viewer to make the "mental leap" to think in a different dimension / proportion than the standard presented in a picture or graph. This requires a more intense thought process, since the proportions are not always easily understood. This is the case here: The viewer / reader can not easily / intuitively ascertain the quantitative relation between normal and magnified level.
